subject: sports-medicine
title: Sports injuries
preambleMarkdown:
# Sports injuries



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Sports injuries is an injury, fracture, dislocation, tendon, ligament, or trauma topic in sports medicine.
- This article focuses on mechanism, return-to-play, rehabilitation, prevention, imaging, and red-flag injury.
- The practical clinical priority is mechanical damage, stability, neurovascular risk, and functional recovery.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Sports injuries to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Sports injuries is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is mechanical damage, stability, neurovascular risk, and functional recovery.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Open versus closed injury.
- Stable versus unstable pattern.
- Neurovascularly intact versus compromised.
- Low-energy, high-energy, overuse, pathologic, pediatric, or older-adult fragility mechanism.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Sports injuries depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In sports medicine, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Sports injuries.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Sports injuries disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Sports injuries should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Sports injuries first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Sports injuries, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pain, swelling, deformity, bruising, loss of function, mechanical symptoms, and mechanism guide suspicion.
- Document distal pulses, capillary refill, sensation, motor function, skin integrity, and compartment tension.
- High-energy trauma requires a broader search for associated injuries.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Sports injuries, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused sports medicine examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Sports injuries presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Sports injuries with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Sports injuries is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Sports injuries should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Provide analgesia, immobilize, image appropriately, and reassess neurovascular status before and after intervention.
- Reduce urgent dislocations or displaced injuries when indicated and safe.
- Escalate open injury, compartment syndrome, vascular compromise, infection, unstable fracture, or failed reduction.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Sports injuries is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Immobilization, elevation, ice, protected weight bearing, rehabilitation, and return-to-activity planning are often central.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Reduction, splinting, casting, aspiration, injection, wound care, fixation, or repair may be needed depending on stability and neurovascular status.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Sports injuries, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Sports injuries may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Sports injuries patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Sports injuries, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Sports injuries in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Sports injuries, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Sports injuries does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Sports injuries may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Sports injuries, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Sports injuries as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Neurovascular examination before and after reduction is essential.
- Open injuries need urgent antibiotics, tetanus review, irrigation, and surgical input.
- Compartment syndrome is a clinical emergency; normal pulses do not exclude it.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Sports injuries draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

```text
SPORTS MEDICINE
I. General Principles
A. History and physical
l . A thorough history and physical are essential to make the diagnosis. Important
aspects of the history include the mechanism of injury as well as the overall
health status of the patient.

2. These are often soft-tissue injuries . Most commonly, ligaments, cartilage ,
tendons, and muscle insertions are injured around joints . These joints must
be examined for instability and resulting loss of range of motion.

II. Anterior Cruciate Ligament [ACLJ Injuries
A. Introduction
l . The ACL prevents anterior translation of tibia with respect to femur. It is an
intra-articular ligament of the knee.

## 2 . These injuries are commonly associated with lateral meniscal tears

(approximately 50%) and medial collateral ligament (MCL) tears.

## 3 . ACL origin : lateral femoral condyle

4. ACL insertion: anterior tibia
B. History and physical
l . Mechanism of injury is a noncontact twisting injury to the knee (soccer and
basketball players) .

2. Patients state they felt a "pop" deep within knee and have immediate swelling
and hemarthrosis (blood within j oint) .

3 . Commonly, a significant knee effusion is present.

4. Lachman test is the most sensitive exam for ACL tear: With knee in 30 degrees
of flexion and hamstrings relaxed, stabilize distal femur in one hand and trans
late the proximal tibia anterior with other hand. A positive test reveals no good
endpoint and is asymmetric with contralateral extremity.

C h i l d a b u s e s h o u l d b e
s u s p e cted for a n y fractures
in c h i l d re n , and it is your
r e s p o n s i b i l ity to r e p o rt
s u s p e cted a b u s e .

Patients w i t h A C L t e a r s
u s u a l l y h e a r a p o p i n the
k n e e and have a n i m m e d i ate
knee h e m a rthrosis. T h e
L a c h m a n t e s t is the m o st
s e n sitive exam find i n g .

C. Diagnosis
l . This is a clinical diagnosis based on history and physical.

2. Radiographs may reveal Segond fracture, an avulsion fracture of lateral
proximal tibia, which is pathognomonic of an ACL tear.

3. MRI is usually used to confirm the diagnosis. Primary utility of MRI is to
preoperatively rule out additional pathology such as meniscal tears that may
require surgical attention (Fig. 1 5 - 1 0 ) .

D . Treatment
l . Clinic or ED disposition: Place patient in knee immobilizer in full extension.

They can bear weight with brace but may require crutches. Orthopedic follow
up within 1 week. If high-energy mechanism or concern for knee dislocation,
perform pulse exam and ankle-brachial index to rule out vascular injury.

2. Nonsurgical treatment (bracing and physical therapy) is reserved for low-demand
patients or patients with little or no instability.

3. Surgical treatment is reserved for active patients with signs of instability.

The ACL is reconstructed with either autograft (patient's patellar or hamstring
tendons) or allograft (cadaver tendons) . This procedure is generally performed
arthroscopically.

Anterior cruciate l igament tear.

P A : Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2004.)

O R T H O P E D I C S U R G E R Y e 2 8 1
Ill. Posterior Cruciate Ligament lPCLJ Injuries
A. Introduction
l . The PCL prevents posterior translation of the tibia with respect to the femur.

2. These injuries are often missed at first presentation.

3. P CL origin: medial femoral condyle
4. P CL insertion: posterior tibia
B. History and physical
l . Mechanism of injury is a posteriorly directed force to anterior proximal tibia
with a flexed knee.

2 . Physical exam acutely will be limited secondary pain and swelling.

3. Posterior drawer test: With the knee in 90 degrees of flexion and stabilizing
patient's foot with body, place posteriorly directed force at proximal tibia. A pos
itive test reveals no good endpoint and asymmetry with contralateral extremity.

C. Diagnosis
l . This is a clinical diagnosis based on history and physical exam.

2. Radiographs usually are negative.

3. MRI is used to confirm the diagnosis if planning surgery. The primary utility
of MRI is to preoperatively rule out any additional pathology, such as meniscal
tears, that may require surgical attention.

D. Treatment
l . Clinic or ED disposition: Place patient in knee immobilizer in full extension.

They can bear weight with brace but may require crutches. Orthopedic follow
up within l week. If high-energy mechanism or concern for knee dislocation,
perform pulse exam and ankle-brachial index to rule out vascular injury.

2. Nonsurgical treatment (bracing) is mainstay of treatment for isolated low- and
medium-grade PCL tears. Surgical indications include complete tears and tears
associated with other ligamentous injuries.

3. Surgical treatment is reserved for active patients with signs of instability.

The PCL is reconstructed with either autograft (patient's tendons) or allograft
(cadaver tendons) . This procedure is generally performed arthroscopically.

IV. Meniscal Tears
A. Introduction
l . The menisci are C-shaped collagenous structures that function as shock
absorbers and secondary j oint stabilizers in the knee j oint.

2. These are extremely common injuries.

3. Medial tears (usually degenerative) are more common than lateral tears
(usually associated with acute ACL injury) .

B. History and physical
l . There may be a history of major or minor traumatic events for acute injuries.

2 . Degenerative tears tend to present with insidious onset of pain.

3. Pain is localized over the medial or lateral joint line that may be worse with tsting
motions or with deep knee flexion (increased forces through meniscus in flexion) .

4. These injuries may present with mechanical symptoms (locking or catching)
of knee.

5. joint line tenderness on palpation is almost always present.

6. McMurray test: While bringing the knee Xom extension to flexion and placing
an external rotation and valgus force, a pop or click will be felt for medial
meniscal tears. An internal rotation and varus force should be used for lateral
meniscal tears. If this only reproduces pain, many surgeons generally still
consider this a positive test.

C. Diagnosis
l . History and physical are significant for raising suspicion of a meniscus injury.

2. Radiographs usually are negative. They may be used to evaluate for arthritic
changes of the knee, which are common with degenerative tears.

3 . MRI: Linear signals extending through two surfaces of menisci indicate a tear.

Although this is necessary to conHrm diagnosis for suspected acute injuries ,
it is not as paramount for suspected degenerative tears in presence of arthritic
changes of knee (Fig. 1 5- l l ) .

P C L tears a r e c o m m o n with
" d a s h b o a rd i n j u r i e s " w h e n
a f l e x e d k n e e s u s t a i n s a
p o ste r i o r l y d i rected force to
proxi m a l t i b i a .

D . Treatment
l . Clinic or ED disposition: They can bear weight with brace but may require
crutches. If acute, a knee brace may be used for pain control. Nonemergent
orthopedic follow-up is acceptable. MRI should be ordered at the discretion
of the orthopedist.

2. Symptomatic treatment consists of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs
(NSAIDs) , rest, and physical therapy for degenerative tears .

## 3 . Surgical treatment is considered for acute tears in young, active patients who

have failed nonsurgical treatment and have no or minimal signs of arthritic
changes. Arthroscopic treatment consists of meniscal debridement or repair
depending on patient characteristics and meniscal pathology.

V. Patellar Dislocation
A. Introduction
l . The patella is the largest sesamoid bone (intratendinous bone) in the body.

It increases leverage and force transmission of the quadriceps for knee
extension.

## 2 . The patella dislocates (generally laterally) from the trochlear groove in the

distal femur.

3. Medial patellofemoral ligament (MPFL) : primary restraint to lateral dislocation
4. Associated with women, ligamentous laxity, and increased Q angle (angle
between line from anterior superior iliac spine [ASIS] to central patella and
line Xom central patella to tibial tubercle)
B. History and physical
l . Mechanism of injury is a noncontact twisting injury to the knee with the knee
in extension.

## 2 . Most dislocations will reduce with reflexive quadriceps contraction prior to

examination.

3. Physical exam will be limited secondary pain and swelling.

4. A signiHcant knee effusion is present, and tenderness to palpation medially
(MPFL)
C. Diagnosis
l . This may be a clinical diagnosis based on history and physical, especially if the
patella spontaneously reduced prior to presentation.

O R T H O P E D I C S U R G E R Y e 283

## 2 . Radiographs may reveal associated lateral femoral condyle or medial patella

facet fracture. Ensure patella is reduced in trochlear groove and rule out loose
intra-articular bodies.

3. MRI: Not routinely ordered. Used only to confirm no loose bodies or fractures.

Additionally, if considering surgical procedures , would be used to confirm
MPFL tear and to plan reconstruction.

D. Treatment
l . Clinic or ED disposition: After appropriate pain control, reduce patella into
trochlear groove and confirm radiographically. Place patient in knee immo
bilizer in full extension. They can bear weight with brace but may require
crutches. Nonemergent orthopedic follow-up is acceptable.

2. Nonsurgical treatment (NSAIDs, temporary immobilization, and physical therapy)
is appropriate for the majority of patients, especially acute first-time dislocators.

3. Surgery is indicated to remove intra-articular loose bodies , and Xacture Hxa
tion may be warranted for associated fractures. MPFL autograft or allograft
reconstructions or bony procedures could be indicated for chronic dislocators.

VI. Lateral Epicondylitis ["Tennis Elbow"]
A. Introduction
l . Although seen with tennis players, this is an overuse injury associated with
repetitive forearm pronation and supination with the elbow extended.

2. Most commonly associated with tendinosis and inflammation at origin of extensor
carpi radialis brevis (ECRB) muscle on the lateral epicondyle of the distal humerus.

B. History and physical
l . Repetitive forearm rotational movement during athletics or work (tennis players
and manual laborers) will cause pain over the lateral humeral epicondyle.

## 2 . Tenderness to palpation is present at the origin of the extensor wad on the

lateral humerus.

3 . Pain is present with resisted wrist extension with the elbow extended.

C. Diagnosis
l . This is a clinical diagnosis based on history and physical exam.

2. Radiographs will be negative but may be used to rule out alternative pathology.

D . Treatment
l . Clinic or ED disposition: Common diagnosis that may be treated at Hrst in
primary care setting. Referral to orthopedics with continued symptoms or
concern for alternate pathology.

2. Nonsurgical treatment consists of activity modiHcation, NSAIDs, and potential
physical therapy to strengthen extensor muscles. Modifications to activities
can include a larger grip (racquet or hammer) , lower racquet tension, and a
higher flexibility racquet.

## 3 . A tension strap worn around proximal forearm may alleviate symptoms and

should be the first line of treatment. This is successful up to 95% of the time.

4. Steroid inj ections may be performed by an orthopedist for recalcitrant cases to
decrease inflammation at the origin of the ECRB (maximum of three injections) .

## 5 . Surgical intervention consists of releasing the E CRB at the insertion on the

lateral epicondyle and debriding the inflamed tissue. This is reserved for
recalcitrant cases after l year of failed nonoperative treatment.

VII. Plantar Fasciitis
A. Introduction
l . The plantar fascia (PF) is a thin, longitudinal connective tissue layer
supporting the arch of the foot that originates on the plantar calcaneus.

2. This disorder is also associated with Achilles tendonitis.

## 3 . May develop from chronic repetitive overuse syndrome resulting from

microtears in the PF
B. History and physical
l . The hallmark symptom is sharp , stabbing, plantar (bottom) surface heel pain
with first steps, especially in the morning or after prolonged inactivity.

P a i n with r e p etitive wrist
exte n s i o n such a s t e n n i s
p l ayers is i n d i c ative of
lateral e p i c o n d y l itis. The
p a t h o l o g y is i n the E C R B
m u s c l e o r i g i n .

P a i n f u l h e e l p a i n t h a t is
worse with the first step
i n the m o r n i n g is a c l a s s i c
f i n d i n g for p l a nt a r fasciitis.

Stretc h i n g is first- l i n e
treatment.

 	-

2. Heel pain improves with movement but worsens by the end of the day.

3. Tenderness to palpation over the proximal plantar calcaneus is often present.

C. Diagnosis
l . This is a clinical diagnosis based on history and physical exam findings.

2. Radiographs may reveal heel spurs at PF or Achilles insertion.

D . Treatment
l . Clinic or ED disposition: Diagnosis and treatment can be performed by pri
mary care physicians. Refer to orthopedics if suspect alternative diagnosis or
for recalcitrant disease.

## 2 . Mainstay of nonsurgical treatment is PF and Achilles specific stretching

exercises to be performed five times daily. Symptom relief can take up to
6 months. Cushioned heel inserts can be prescribed for symptomatic control.

NSAIDs may be prescribed for pain control.

3. Alternative treatments include corticosteroid inj ections, high-intensity
ultrasound, and surgical release. These should be reserved for patients with
symptoms lasting longer than 9 months under a supervised care program.

VIII. Achilles Tendonitis
A. Introduction
l . The Achilles tendon is constituted of the gastrocnemius and soleus tendons,
whose function is as the primary plantar flexor of the ankle.

## 2 . Chronic repetitive overuse syndrome of Achilles insertion into calcaneus can

lead to tendonitis .

3 . Middle-aged patients are most commonly affected.

4. Can be associated with plantar fasciitis
B. History and physical
l . The hallmark symptom is sharp , stabbing, posterior surface heel pain that
worsens with increased activity and dorsiflexion of ankle.

2 . Tenderness to palpation is present over the posterior calcaneus.

## 3 . Thompson test: In prone position with knee flexed, squeeze proximal calf

and evaluate for ankle plantar flexion. If asymmetric with contralateral side,
consider Achilles tendon rupture.

C. Diagnosis
l . This is a clinical diagnosis based on history and physical exam.

2. Radiographs may reveal heel spurs at PF or Achilles insertion.

D . Treatment
Hand
l. Clinic or ED disposition: Diagnosis and treatment can be performed by pri
mary care physicians. Refer to orthopedics if suspect alternative diagnosis or
for recalcitrant disease. If acute onset of pain with a palpable gap in the tendon
is present, consider Achilles tendon rupture with urgent orthopedic referral.

## 2 . Mainstay of nonsurgical treatment is Achilles tendon-specific stretching

exercises and strengthening exercises. A period of nonweight bearing
in a short-leg walking cast or boot walker may be necessary to decrease
inflammation. This disease can have a prolonged course. NSAIDs may be
prescribed for pain control.

## 3 . Surgical debridement of the Achilles tendon only considered for patients who

fail nonsurgical treatment as described earlier for extended periods of time,
who have concurrent abnormal calcaneal anatomy, or who have rupture of the
Achilles tendon.

I. General Principles
A. Hand disorders include congenital deformities, acquired deformities, trauma, and
nerve compression injuries .

B. History and physical
l . There are many complex biomechanics of the function of the bones, j oints ,
and tendons of the hand. A detailed physical exam can help to determine
which of the many anatomical structures may be causing pathology.

O R T H O P E D I C S U R G E R Y e 285
II. Ganglion Cyst of the Wrist
A. Introduction
l. A ganglion is a mucinous-filled cyst adjacent to a joint capsule or tendon sheath
that contains clear and colorless fluid ( 70% on dorsal wrist, 30% on volar wrist) .

2. These are more common in women than men.

3. They are the most common soft-tissue mass of the hand.

B. History and physical
l . This often presents as a slow-growing, painful mass about the wrist that may
fluctuate in size and appearance.

## 2 . These cysts are firm and well-circumscribed superficial masses, usually on the

dorsum of the wrist.

3. Mass will transilluminate with a pen light.

4. Tenderness may be present over cysts with palpation.

C. Diagnosis
l . This is a clinical diagnosis based on history and physical exam.

2. Radiographs are nondiagnostic. Advanced imaging studies are not usually
necessary.

D. Treatment
l . Clinic or ED disposition: Diagnosis and treatment can be performed by
primary care physicians. Refer to orthopedics if suspect alternative diagnosis
or for concern regarding other soft-tissue masses or tumors .

2. Many patients will resolve the cyst without intervention, so observation may
be warranted depending on the scope of symptoms. Aspiration of dorsal cysts
with placement of a compressive dressing and brief immobilization has a 50%
recurrence rate but is diagnostic and avoids surgery in many patients. Avoid
volar aspiration, given proximity of radial artery. NSAIDs may be prescribed
for pain control.

3. Surgical excision of the cyst is indicated for recalcitrant, symptomatic dorsal
cysts or symptomatic volar cysts . It is important to remove the complete stalk
of the cysts from the j oint for decreased recurrence rates.

Ill. Boxer's Fracture
A. Introduction
l . Boxer's fractures are defined as fractures of the fifth metacarpal neck.

2. These are common in younger males, often from closed-fist punching injuries.

3. Although usually a closed injury, they can be open fractures depending on impact.

B. History and physical
l . Mechanism of injury is trauma, likely an impact to a closed fist. If an open
fracture is present, question what the patient struck during the trauma.

2 . Tenderness to palpation is present over the ulnar border of the hand.

3. Perform a neurovascular exam on Hngertip .

4. Evaluate for any open wounds , which may be concerning for open fracture.

These are often termed "Hght bites" because the fist commonly strikes an
opponent's teeth.

C. Diagnosis
l . This is a radiographic diagnosis. Three radiographic views of the hand are
preferred (Fig. 1 5 - 1 2 ) .

2 . The angulation of the Xacture i s measured on A P and lateral radiographs.

Evaluate for additional fractures and Xacture into the metacarpophalangeal
(MCP) joint. Subcutaneous air indicates open injuries . Acceptable angulation
for nonsurgical treatment is up to 60 degrees of angulation.

D . Treatment
l . Clinic or ED disposition: Manual reduction of fracture after local anesthetic
inj ection is commonly performed in the emergency department. The patient is
then placed into an ulnar gutter splint in intrinsic plus position (MCP flexed
and interphalangeal j oints extended) . Patients should follow up with orthopedic
surgeon within l week. Maintain splint or short-arm ulnar gutter cast until the
fracture is healed at 4 to 6 weeks.

## 2 . If an open fracture is present, the patient will need an urgent orthopedic

surgical consultation along with tetanus and antibiotic administration.

3. Surgical intervention may be warranted for closed fractures if closed reduction,
and percutaneous pinning (CRPP) may be required for patients with residual
angulation greater than 60 degrees or if combined with other hand fractures or
dislocations.

IV. Carpal Tunnel Syndrome lCTSJ
A. Introduction

## 1 . This is a compression neuropathy of the median nerve (most common

compression neuropathy) within the carpal tunnel of the wrist.

## 2 . Some believe it is caused by patients who perform repetitive activities of the

wrist.

3. It is also associated with diabetes mellitus, pregnancy, and rheumatoid
arthritis.

4. The carpal tunnel consists of nine wrist and finger flexors, as well as the
median nerve.

B. History and physical

## 1 . Patients describe numbness and tingling in radial three and a half fingers that

is worse at night.

2. The carpal tunnel compression test, performed by compressing the carpal
tunnel with examiner's fingers, is the most sensitive test. A positive test
results in reproduction of symptoms within 30 seconds of administration of
pressure.

C. Diagnosis

## 1 . This is a clinical diagnosis based on history and physical exam. Radiographs

are unnecessary and will be negative if CTS is the correct diagnosis.

2. Electromyography (EMG) and nerve conduction studies (N CS) may be used to
ensure accurate diagnosis in difficult cases. They would show increased nerve
latencies, nerve conduction velocities, and decreased motor strength.

D . Treatment

## 1 . Clinic or ED disposition: Although most patients will be diagnosed by primary

physicians, an elective orthopedic hand referral is appropriate.

2. Nonsurgical treatments include the use of wrist splints both during activity
and at night to prevent extremes of motion (both flexion and extension) ,
NSAIDs, and activity modification. Steroid injections into the carpal tunnel
can be performed for both diagnostic and therapeutic reasons. If positive result
with inj ection, prognosis is better for surgical procedures .

## 3 . Open or endoscopic carpal tunnel release is commonly performed and is indi

cated for patients with refractory disease or who present with atrophy of their
thenar eminence, a sign of long-standing disease.

O R T H O P E D I C S U R G E R Y e 2 8 7
Spine
I. General Principles
A. History and physical
l . A detailed history and neurologic exam is required for any patient presenting
with a potential spine injury or disorder. This allows the practitioner to
delineate which levels of the spine may be auected. This exam includes motor,
sensory, and reflex exams.

2. Any detectable neurologic deHcit requires urgent consultation with spine surgery.

II. Lumbar Spinal Stenosis
A. Introduction
l . Reduction in spinal canal volume causes spinal nerve root compression at the
cauda equina (the spinal cord usually ends at level Ll to L2 in adults ) .

## 2 . M o s t commonly caused b y degenerative changes t o bone, disc, or ligamentous

anatomy of spine
3. A more acute presentation could be secondary to lumbar disc herniation.

B. History and physical
l . Low back, buttock, and leg pain (neurogenic claudication) will be present.

2. Symptoms worsen with lumbar extension (standing) and improve with lumbar
flexion (sitting, leaning forward) .

## 3 . Neurogenic claudication symptoms slowly resolve with rest, whereas vascular

claudication symptoms resolve more quickly with rest.

4. Symptoms are often bilateral but may be unilateral.

5 . Perform vascular exam (ankle-brachial index) to rule out vascular claudication.

C. Diagnosis
l . Distinguish neurogenic claudication from vascular claudication based on
history and physical exam.

2. APfflateral lumbar spine radiographs will show degenerative changes of the
spine, including disc space narrowing and osteophyte formation.

3. MRI should be ordered to assess extent and location of the stenosis. If MRI is
unobtainable, then a CT myelogram may be a sufficient examination.

4. Diagnosis is based both on clinical and radiographic findings.

D. Treatment
l . Clinic or ED disposition: Rarely will patients present with cauda equina syn
drome (acute spinal nerve root compression causing urinary retention, fecal
incontinence, and saddle anesthesia) . This would require emergent surgical
consultation and MRI. Otherwise, patients may be referred to an orthopedic
spine surgeon electively.

2. Nonsurgical treatments include anti-inflammatory medications, physical ther
apy for flexio14extension strengthening, weight loss, and activity modification.

3. Epidural steroid inj ections may be indicated for some patients at the spine sur
geon's discretion with an opportunity to alleviate symptoms obviating surgical
intervention. Lumbar bracing may help pain.

4. Surgical treatment is for patients who have failed conservative care. Indications
are continued neurogenic claudication and/or cauda equina syndrome. Back
pain is not an indication for surgical intervention. Surgery consists of decom
pression of appropriate spinal levels .

Ill. Lumbar Disc Herniation
A. Introduction
l. Disc herniations are acute or chronic tears in the disc annulus that can lead to
extrusion of the nucleosis pulposus, causing impingement of lumbar spinal roots.

2. Most herniations are asymptomatic, and most asymptomatic herniations
resolve without any treatment.

3. They are more common in males and occur most Xequently between the ages
of 30 and 50 years old.

4. Ninety-Hve percent of disc herniations are at L4/L5 and L5/S l discs, with the
latter being more common.

5. Paracentral disc herniation will affect traversing nerve root (nerve root from
level below disc) , whereas far lateral disc herniation will affect exiting nerve
root (nerve from level above disc) as seen in Figure 1 5 - 1 3 .

B. History and physical

## 1 . Most commonly, these patients will present with sciatica (radicular extremity

pain) , but beware of cauda equina syndrome.

## 2 . A full neurologic examination will be needed but deficits would be as seen in

Clinical Pearl 1 5-6.

## 3 . A straight-leg raise is positive with reproduction of radicular pain between

30 and 70 degrees of hip flexion with knee fully extended.

C. Diagnosis

## 1 . Lumbar spine radiographs should be ordered to rule out other diagnoses and

may show disc space narrowing or other degenerative changes.

2 . MRI without gadolinium will confirm diagnosis and show anatomy.

3 . The diagnosis is based both on clinical and radiographic findings.

D. Treatment

## 1 . Clinic or ED disposition: Primary care physicians will diagnose and manage

condition regularly. Nonemergent orthopedic referral for continued symptoms
despite nonsurgical treatment. MRI to be ordered if concern for tumor, infec
tion, high-energy trauma, cauda equina syndrome, or persistent symptoms .

2. Nonsurgical treatments are highly successful and include anti-inflammatory
medications (NSAIDs or steroids) , physical therapy for flexio14extension
strengthening, lumbar stretching exercises, weight loss, and activity
modiHcation. Bed rest is not appropriate.

3. Nerve root block with steroid inj ections may be used for refractory or severe
cases for pain control. These can also be diagnostic and prognostic for surgical
interventions.

4. Lumbar discectomy (remove the extruded disc from the spinal canal) would
be considered for large herniations and patients with recalcitrant symptoms.

These procedures are most helpful for radicular pain and may not alleviate the
back pain component of symptoms.

IV. Compression Fracture
A. Introduction
l . These are vertebral body fractures that are the most common osteoporotic
fracture in the elderly.

2. Osteoporosis is defined by a T -score < - 2 . 5 on dual energy X-ray absorpti
ometry (DEXA) scan. The bone has normal architecture but has signiHcantly
decreased bone mass .

3 . Many patients have pain but never present for evaluation.

4. The fractures are most common in lower thoracic and upper lumbar vertebral bodies.

5. With one fragility fracture, the risk of future fragility fractures (compression fracture,
distal radius fracture, and femoral neck fracture) increases significantly over time.

B. History and physical
l . Patient may give history of very minor trauma such as twisting in chair or
lifting small child.

2. Back pain and tenderness to palpation will be present at area of compression
fracture.

3. Rarely will these present with any neurologic deHcit.

4. Make sure to elicit personal and family history of cancer, constitutional
symptoms, or infection.

C . Diagnosis
l . AP and lateral standing radiographs of entire spine should be ordered because
other noncontiguous Xactures are commonly identiHed (Fig. 1 5 - 14) .

2. Lab work to rule out infection or tumor may be necessary.

3. Although not commonly needed, MRI with gadolinium should be ordered
for atypical fractures, such as fractures that include upper thoracic vertebrae,
fractures not consistent with patient's age and mechanism of injury, or if there
is concern for infection anor tumor.

D. Treatment
l . Clinic or ED disposition: Although most patients will be diagnosed by primary
physicians, an elective orthopedic spine referral is appropriate for most patients.

2. Nonsurgical treatments include anti-inflammatory medications , physical ther
apy strengthening and stretching once acute pain has decreased, and activity
modiHcation. Some patients may Hnd bracing effective for pain control. With
osteoporotic fractures , medical management of osteoporosis is indicated by
either a primary care physician or rheumatologist.

3. Surgical treatment is indicated for patients who have failed a 6-week course
of the nonsurgical treatments described earlier without symptom relief.

Kyphoplasty, inj ection of cement into the vertebral body, may be performed at
that time but is only indicated for pain relief because this procedure does not
correct kyphotic deformity.
```
